questions.question: A bag contains 5 red marbles, 3 blue marbles, and 2 green marbles. If you draw one marble at random, what is the probability that it is either red or green?
questions.explanation: Total marbles: 5 + 3 + 2 = 10. Red or green marbles: 5 + 2 = 7. Probability = 7/10.

questions.question:
The table below shows the distribution of 200 students by grade level and whether they play a sport.

| Grade | Play Sport | Don't Play Sport | Total |
|-------|-----------|------------------|-------|
| 9     | 35        | 45               | 80    |
| 10    | 40        | 80               | 120   |
| Total | 75        | 125              | 200   |

If a student is selected at random, what is the probability the student is in grade 9 AND plays a sport?
questions.explanation: Students in grade 9 who play sport: 35. Total students: 200. Probability = 35/200 = 7/40.

questions.question: A fair six-sided die is rolled twice. What is the probability that the sum of the two rolls is 7?
questions.explanation: Pairs summing to 7: (1,6), (2,5), (3,4), (4,3), (5,2), (6,1) = 6 outcomes. Total outcomes: 6 × 6 = 36. Probability = 6/36 = 1/6.

questions.question: In a class, 60% of students passed the math test. Of those who passed, 75% also passed the science test. What is the probability that a randomly selected student passed both tests?
questions.explanation: P(both) = P(passed math) × P(passed science | passed math) = 0.60 × 0.75 = 0.45.

questions.question: A deck of standard playing cards contains 52 cards. If you draw two cards without replacement, what is the probability that both cards are hearts?
A spinner is divided into 4 equal sections colored red, blue, yellow, and green. You spin it twice. What is the probability that you get the same color twice?
questions.explanation: P(same color) = P(RR) + P(BB) + P(YY) + P(GG) = (1/4)² × 4 = 4/16 = 1/4. Or: first spin any color (prob 1), second spin must match (prob 1/4).
